title: McMahon Grilled on Education Cuts: 'Spitting Mad' Parents and a Civil Rights Office in Crisis
summary: Education Secretary Linda McMahon walked into a Senate hearing Tuesday and faced a barrage of criticism over her aggressive efforts to shrink the very department she leads. The hearing, ostensibly about the White House's 2027 education budget, quickly became a referendum on McMahon's tenure—and a flashpoint over the future of civil rights enforcement in schools. At the center of the storm is the Office for Civil Rights (OCR), which investigates discrimination complaints based on race, sex, disability, and more. Last year, the administration fired more than half of OCR's lawyers and staff and shuttered seven of its 12 regional offices. McMahon initially framed the cuts as a move toward "efficiency" and "accountability." But on Tuesday, she denied responsibility, calling the decision "hindsight" and promising to hire more lawyers to tackle a backlog of thousands of cases. Democratic Senator Chris Murphy of Connecticut wasn't buying it. He recounted the story of a student with autism who had been restrained and secluded at her school. OCR was investigating until McMahon took office and the local field office was closed, effectively ending the probe. "Well you fired half the department," Murphy pressed. McMahon's proposed budget would slash OCR's funding by $49 million, even as she claims to be rebuilding it. The hearing also zeroed in on special education. The budget proposes a $539 million increase for the Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA), bringing total funding to $16 billion. But McMahon confirmed that her department is considering moving IDEA oversight to either the Department of Labor or Health and Human Services (HHS). Advocates argue that placing special education in HHS would frame disabilities as a health concern rather than an educational one, potentially undermining students' right to a "free appropriate public education." Senator Patty Murray of Washington said parents are "spitting mad" because they fear their children's education will take a backseat to medical labels. McMahon pushed back, insisting that "shifting authority back to the states will not come at the expense of essential federal programs" and that students would be "treated the same way as before." But with no final decision announced, uncertainty hangs over millions of families. Republican Senator John Kennedy praised McMahon's push for school choice, but even some GOP members expressed concern about proposed cuts to TRIO, a suite of programs that help disadvantaged students enter and complete college. As the hearing ended, Senator Tammy Baldwin pointedly noted the $49 million OCR cut, and McMahon signaled she might be open to adjusting that figure. For now, the clash leaves parents, students, and educators waiting to see whether the department's dismantling will erode the protections they've relied on for decades.
why_it_matters: Decisions made about your school's civil rights office and special education funding directly affect whether you or your classmates will have a safe place to report discrimination, receive the learning support you're entitled to, and access college — shaping the fairness of your entire educational journey.

background_read: The U.S. Department of Education was created in 1979 under President Jimmy Carter, consolidating federal education programs. Since then, several presidents and lawmakers have proposed to abolish it or dramatically shrink it, arguing that education should be controlled locally. The Office for Civil Rights (OCR) was established even earlier — in 1967, in the Department of Health, Education, and Welfare — to enforce Title VI of the Civil Rights Act. Today it handles complaints about race, sex, disability, and more.

Linda McMahon (a former professional wrestling executive, co-founder of WWE, and Trump cabinet member) was confirmed as Education Secretary in March 2025. Her budget plan reflects a long-standing conservative push for school choice and reduced federal oversight. However, the Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA), first passed in 1975, guarantees a 'free appropriate public education' to students with disabilities; moving its oversight out of the Education Department could change how those rights are enforced.

The TRIO programs date back to the 1960s War on Poverty and have long enjoyed bipartisan support. Yet this hearing exposed fractures even among Republicans over cuts that would affect college access for low-income and first-generation students. The mention of a student being restrained and secluded also echoes a broader national concern: in 2020, the Government Accountability Office found widespread underreporting of such incidents, making OCR investigations crucial for student safety.
Article_Structure: LEAD: Education Secretary faces angry lawmakers over deep cuts.
└─ specific: The hearing on the 2027 budget morphs into a trial of her tenure.
KEY ISSUE 1: Civil rights enforcement gutted
├─ WHAT: OCR lost most of its staff and offices; budget to be cut $49 million.
├─ ADMIN RESPONSE: McMahon calls the staffing cuts 'hindsight,' promises to hire.
└─ EVIDENCE: Senator Murphy's story of a restrained student shows real harm.
KEY ISSUE 2: Special education's uncertain future
├─ PROPOSAL: IDEA funding increase of $539 million, but idea to move oversight to HHS or Labor.
├─ PARENT ANGER: Senator Murray says parents are 'spitting mad.'
└─ WORRY: Disability as health concern might weaken 'educational' rights.
├─ GOP Senator Kennedy praises school choice push.
└─ CONCERN: Even some Republicans uneasy about cutting TRIO college-access programs.
UNRESOLVED: McMahon signals openness to adjust OCR cut, but no final decisions.
└─ STAKES: Millions of students' protections hang in the balance.

perspectives.description: McMahon and allies argue that reducing the federal footprint returns power to states and local communities, where they believe education decisions belong. They frame cuts as 'efficiency' and 'accountability,' and claim that core programs won't suffer.

By slashing the office that investigates discrimination, students facing racial harassment, sex-based bullying, or unfair disability treatment lose their most important federal backstop. The closure of regional offices means fewer eyes on local schools — and real cases, like the autistic student's, fall through the cracks.

The fight over federal versus state control of education is as old as the Department itself. Proposals to shrink or abolish it have surfaced in every Republican administration since Reagan. The tension between 'equity' mandates from Washington and 'local control' has never been resolved, and this budget is the latest chapter in that long tug-of-war.

If IDEA moves to HHS, the entire legal framework for special education could shift — perhaps requiring medical diagnoses to access classroom support, or making it harder for parents to sue. TRIO cuts might reduce the number of first-generation college-goers. The next election and court challenges will determine whether these changes become permanent.
